title: Immigrant Justice Corps 2026 Community Fellowship: Fully Funded 3-Year Opportunity for Aspiring Immigration Advocates
summary: The 2026 Community Fellowship by Immigrant Justice Corps (IJC) stands as one of the most impactful and prestigious opportunities for young professionals passionate about immigration justice, social equity, and legal advocacy in the United States. As the nation’s first and only fellowship program dedicated exclusively to immigration, IJC continues to bridge the justice gap by placing talented graduates into leading legal service organizations. With applications now open for its twelfth cohort, this fully funded, three-year fellowship offers a transformative career pathway for individuals committed to supporting low-income immigrant communities while gaining hands-on legal experience. What is the IJC Community Fellowship? The IJC Community Fellowship is a structured, paid program designed to recruit recent college graduates and immerse them in frontline immigration advocacy. Fellows are placed within highly respected legal service organizations, where they directly support underserved immigrant populations. Currently, IJC hosts over 130 fellows across more than 30 U.S. states, contributing significantly to expanding access to justice. The 2026 cohort introduces a unique opportunity with three-year placements , allowing fellows to develop expertise, build professional networks, and achieve formal accreditation in immigration legal practice. Fellowship Structure and Professional Development The Community Fellowship is carefully designed to provide both practical experience and professional certification. Accreditation Pathway Fellows are expected to progress through the U.S. Department of Justice Recognition Accreditation Program: Partial Accreditation : Achieved within the first year (ideally within 6–12 months), enabling fellows to represent clients before U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services. Full Accreditation : Targeted between 12–18 months, allowing fellows to practice before Immigration Courts. This structured pathway ensures that fellows graduate with strong legal competencies and recognized credentials. Key Responsibilities of Community Fellows Participants in the program take on meaningful and impactful roles, including: Managing individual caseloads under legal supervision Providing direct legal services and representation to immigrants Translating complex immigration policies into accessible language Supporting communities through culturally competent advocacy Engaging with diverse populations facing legal and systemic barriers This immersive experience equips fellows with both technical legal skills and deep community engagement expertise. Fellowship Benefits The IJC Community Fellowship offers a comprehensive benefits package, making it one of the most competitive programs in the field: Full-time salary Employee benefits Extensive training and mentorship Hands-on legal experience Professional accreditation pathway Long-term career development in immigration law and advocacy Placement Locations For the 2026 cohort, placements are expected to be based in: New York City Long Island Upstate New York Fellows must be willing to relocate to their assigned location. While some placements may offer hybrid work arrangements, fully remote participation is not available. Eligibility Criteria Applicants must meet the following requirements: Be current undergraduate students graduating by Spring 2026 or recent graduates (no earlier than Spring 2024) Hold an Associate or Bachelor’s degree Be eligible for work authorization in the United States for the full duration of the fellowship Demonstrate proficiency in English and at least one additional language Spanish (including indigenous dialects) is strongly preferred Applicants with DACA or TPS status are encouraged to apply. Application Components Candidates must submit a complete application package including: Completed application form Resume or CV (maximum two pages) Unofficial academic transcripts Statement of Interest (maximum 500 words) Essay on immigration policy (maximum 500 words) At least one letter of recommendation (two recommended) The written components are critical and should reflect the applicant’s passion, cultural awareness, and commitment to immigration justice. Selection Process The selection process is competitive and takes place in multiple stages: Application Review (Early May 2026) Interviews (Mid-May 2026, virtual format) Final Matching Process (Late May 2026) Fellowship Agreements Issued (June 2026) Successful applicants are matched with host organizations based on mutual preferences and program needs. Application Deadline All application materials, including recommendation letters, must be submitted by: Sunday, May 3, 2026 at 11:59 p.m. (ET) Applicants are strongly encouraged to begin early and maintain close communication with their referees to ensure timely submission. Why Apply for the IJC Community Fellowship? This fellowship is more than a professional opportunity—it is a platform to create meaningful social impact. Fellows become part of a national movement working to ensure equitable access to justice for immigrant communities. By combining legal training, community engagement, and professional mentorship, the IJC Community Fellowship prepares participants for long-term careers in law, policy, and advocacy. title: Apply Now: Paid Remote Climate & Conservation Finance Internship 2026 with The Nature Conservancy
summary: Are you looking to gain hands-on experience in climate finance, policy, and global sustainability? The Climate Conservation Finance Policy Internship 2026 with The Nature Conservancy (TNC) is a paid, fully remote opportunity designed for students and recent graduates eager to work at the intersection of finance, policy, and environmental impact. Over eight weeks, interns will contribute to real-world projects, support global initiatives, and build valuable professional skills. Applications are reviewed on a rolling basis, so early submission is strongly encouraged. About the Opportunity The Nature Conservancy (TNC) is one of the world’s leading environmental organizations, working to protect lands and waters globally. Through its Internships @TNC program , the organization offers structured, paid opportunities for emerging professionals to apply their academic knowledge to real-world conservation challenges. This specific internship is part of the Climate and Conservation Finance Policy (CCFP) team , which focuses on financing climate and nature solutions in emerging markets and developing economies. The team works with governments, financial institutions, and global stakeholders to design and implement innovative financial mechanisms that support sustainability and economic resilience. Interns in this role will contribute to projects that explore tools such as debt-for-nature swaps, green finance frameworks, ecosystem service payments, and climate-related financial strategies . They will also support research and policy development aimed at integrating nature and biodiversity into economic systems and financial decision-making. The internship runs for 8 weeks, from 15 June to 7 August 2026 , and is conducted remotely. Interns will participate in onboarding, training, mentorship, and networking activities, while also completing a capstone project that showcases their learning and contributions. Why This Opportunity Matters Climate change and environmental degradation are among the most pressing global challenges today. Addressing these issues requires innovative financial solutions that can support sustainable development while protecting natural ecosystems. The work of the CCFP team at The Nature Conservancy is critical in advancing these solutions by helping governments and institutions design financial systems that integrate environmental priorities. This internship allows participants to contribute to efforts that have real-world policy and economic implications , particularly in developing regions where the need for sustainable solutions is urgent. By joining this program, interns gain insight into how global environmental strategies are developed and implemented, while also building the skills needed to contribute to long-term sustainability goals. What You’ll Gain This internship offers a strong foundation for careers in sustainability, policy, and international development: Paid Experience: Earn $22 per hour while gaining professional experience in a global organization. Real-World Impact: Work on projects that influence climate and conservation finance strategies worldwide. Skill Development: Build expertise in research, policy writing, data analysis, and stakeholder engagement. Mentorship and Training: Receive guidance from experienced professionals and participate in structured learning sessions. Global Exposure: Contribute to international initiatives and support major events such as climate and biodiversity forums. This opportunity is particularly valuable for individuals aiming to enter careers in climate policy, development finance, or environmental economics . Eligibility To be eligible for this internship, applicants must: Be currently enrolled in an undergraduate or graduate program , or be a recent graduate (within six months) Have a background in policy, finance, economics, environmental studies, or related fields Demonstrate experience in research, writing, and communication Be fluent in English Preferred qualifications include: Experience in climate finance, international development, or public policy Familiarity with data analysis or visualization tools Ability to work independently in a remote and collaborative environment Knowledge of additional languages such as Spanish, Portuguese, or French Application Process and Deadline To apply for the Climate Conservation Finance Policy Internship: Prepare Your Application: Update your CV and ensure your experience aligns with the role requirements. Apply Online: Submit your application through The Nature Conservancy’s official careers portal. Rolling Review: Applications are reviewed on a rolling basis, and the position may close once a strong candidate pool is identified. Internship Duration: 15 June – 7 August 2026 Location: Remote Compensation: $22/hour To learn more and apply, visit the official Nature Conservancy internship job posting . title: Susan Carroll Fellowship 2026: Fully Funded Investigative Journalism Opportunity for Emerging Reporters
summary: The Susan Carroll Fellowship, offered by the Investigative Reporters and Editors (IRE), is a prestigious and highly competitive opportunity designed to empower emerging journalists with the skills, mentorship, and financial support needed to excel in investigative reporting. Named in honor of the late investigative journalist Susan Carroll, this fellowship aims to continue her legacy of impactful, data-driven journalism that holds institutions accountable and amplifies underreported stories. This fellowship is particularly significant in today’s media landscape, where investigative journalism plays a crucial role in uncovering truths, promoting transparency, and driving societal change. The program is structured to support early-career journalists by providing them with access to training, resources, and a network of experienced professionals in the field. Overview of the Susan Carroll Fellowship The Susan Carroll Fellowship is designed for journalists who are passionate about investigative reporting but may lack access to the resources and training needed to fully develop their potential. Through this fellowship, selected participants receive financial assistance to attend IRE’s renowned training programs, where they can gain hands-on experience and learn advanced investigative techniques. The fellowship places a strong emphasis on data journalism, a field that Susan Carroll was deeply committed to. Participants are trained to analyze complex datasets, uncover hidden patterns, and transform raw information into compelling stories that inform and engage the public. What the Fellowship Offers One of the most attractive aspects of the Susan Carroll Fellowship is its comprehensive support package. Fellows receive funding that covers the cost of attending IRE training events, including registration fees, travel expenses, and accommodation. This ensures that financial constraints do not hinder talented journalists from accessing world-class training. In addition to financial support, fellows gain access to: Intensive investigative journalism training sessions Workshops on data analysis, reporting techniques, and storytelling Networking opportunities with leading journalists and media professionals Mentorship from experienced investigative reporters Exposure to cutting-edge tools and technologies used in modern journalism These benefits collectively create a transformative learning experience that equips fellows with the skills needed to produce impactful investigative stories. Training and Learning Experience The fellowship provides an immersive learning environment where participants engage in practical, hands-on training. Workshops are led by industry experts who share real-world insights and best practices. Topics typically include: Data collection and cleaning Statistical analysis and visualization Freedom of information requests Ethical considerations in investigative journalism Digital security for journalists By the end of the program, fellows are expected to have a deeper understanding of investigative reporting processes and the confidence to undertake complex journalistic projects. Application Process and Deadline Applicants are required to submit a detailed application that demonstrates their interest in investigative journalism, their career goals, and how the fellowship will help them achieve those goals. Supporting materials may include writing samples, a resume, and references. Deadline: The application deadline varies annually. Applicants are strongly encouraged to check the official fellowship page and apply early to avoid missing out on this opportunity. Why You Should Apply The Susan Carroll Fellowship is more than just a training program; it is a career-defining opportunity for aspiring investigative journalists. It not only enhances technical skills but also provides a platform for fellows to connect with a global community of professionals who are dedicated to making a difference through journalism. For early-career journalists, this fellowship can serve as a stepping stone to greater opportunities, including positions in leading media organizations, collaborations on high-impact investigations, and recognition within the journalism community.Moreover, the fellowship’s focus on inclusivity ensures that journalists from diverse backgrounds have the opportunity to participate and contribute their unique perspectives to the field.In an era where credible information is more important than ever, initiatives like the Susan Carroll Fellowship play a vital role in strengthening the field of investigative journalism. By investing in the next generation of reporters, the fellowship helps ensure that important stories continue to be told and that accountability remains a cornerstone of society. If you are passionate about uncovering the truth and making a meaningful impact through journalism, the Susan Carroll Fellowship is an opportunity you should not overlook. title: Empowering Women for Climate Action: Sustainable Livelihoods and Community Resilience.
summary: Across the globe, the intersection of climate change and gender inequality continues to shape the lives of millions. By integrating environmental action with economic empowerment, the organization is creating pathways for women to lead, thrive, and contribute meaningfully to their communities. The session was marked by a spirit of collaboration, determination, and hope, as women began their journey toward self-reliance and financial empowerment. This milestone reflects SCWEI’s broader goal of creating inclusive opportunities where women can transform their skills into sustainable livelihoods. title: Apply Now: CBAI Summer AI Safety Fellowship 2026 in the USA (Fully Funded)
summary: The Cambridge Boston Alignment Initiative (CBAI) Summer Research Fellowship in AI Safety 2026 is a prestigious, fully funded program designed for aspiring researchers who want to build impactful careers in artificial intelligence safety. title: Hack-Nation Global AI Hackathon 2026: Build, Innovate, and Compete for $30,000+ in Prizes
summary: The rapid evolution of artificial intelligence has created unprecedented opportunities for developers, innovators, and creators worldwide. One of the most exciting platforms driving this innovation is Hack-Nation’s Global AI Hackathon 2026, a high-impact international event designed to bring together some of the brightest minds in technology to build real-world AI solutions in just 24 hours. As a nonprofit organization, it connects global talent from students to experienced developers and empowers them to turn ideas into functional prototypes under real startup-like pressure. What is the Hack-Nation Global AI Hackathon? The Hack-Nation Global AI Hackathon is an intensive, fast-paced innovation competition where participants collaborate to design, develop, and launch AI-powered solutions within a 24-hour sprint. Unlike traditional competitions, this hackathon emphasizes execution, creativity, and real-world application over theoretical concepts.Participants will work individually or in teams to solve open-ended challenges using artificial intelligence, machine learning, automation tools, and modern web technologies. The event is structured to simulate real startup environments, where speed, innovation, and adaptability are critical.The 2026 edition continues to expand its global footprint, attracting participants from over 60 countries and connecting them through both physical hubs and virtual platforms. Why You Should Participate Participating in the Hack-Nation Global AI Hackathon offers far more than just competition. It is a gateway to global exposure, skill development, and career advancement. Additionally, more than $150,000 worth of API credits will be available during the hackathon, enabling participants to build powerful and scalable AI solutions. Real-World Experience This hackathon is designed to mimic startup conditions. Participants experience the pressure of building and shipping products within tight deadlines, preparing them for real-world tech and entrepreneurial environments. Participants get the opportunity to collaborate with talented individuals from around the world, forming meaningful professional relationships. title: Apply Now: African Union Call for Submissions (Up to $50,000 Grants) for EdTech, TVET, AI and Skills Development
summary: The Department of Education, Science, Technology, and Innovation of the African Union has officially launched the Innovating Education in Africa (IEA) 2026 Call for Submissions , inviting innovators across the continent to propose transformative solutions that address Africa’s most pressing education and skills development challenges. Background: Why Innovation in Education Matters Despite notable progress, education systems across Africa continue to face persistent challenges in access, equity, quality, relevance, and skills alignment . Key statistics highlight the urgency: 20% of children aged 6–11 are out of school 33% of children aged 12–14 are excluded Nearly 60% of youth aged 15–17 remain out of education These challenges disproportionately affect girls and are compounded by: Shortage and uneven distribution of qualified teachers Limited professional development opportunities Weak alignment between education outcomes and labor market needs At higher levels, systems such as Technical and Vocational Education and Training (TVET) and universities face: Outdated curricula and weak industry linkages Limited digital integration and AI adoption Insufficient research capacity and innovation ecosystems To address these issues, Africa needs scalable, cost-effective, and system-integrated innovations that strengthen education systems and align with continental strategies. About Innovating Education in Africa (IEA) Launched in 2018 by the African Union, IEA aims to identify, promote, and scale impactful education innovations across Africa. Impact so far: 1,500+ education stakeholders engaged 180 innovations supported Up to USD 1 million mobilized In 2026, the initiative expands its scope to include: Basic and secondary education Higher education and research ecosystems TVET and skills development Digital systems and AI integration Green skills and sustainability 2026 Thematic Focus Areas IEA 2026 is seeking innovations in the following key areas: 1. Basic and Secondary Education Foundational literacy and numeracy technologies Teacher professional development solutions AI-enabled assessment and adaptive learning Inclusive and gender-responsive education tools 2. Higher Education STI Ecosystems University-industry innovation hubs Research commercialization platforms Digital universities and hybrid learning systems Robotics and STEM centers of excellence Science parks, labs, and incubators 3. TVET and Skills Development AI-integrated training models Robotics and automation training Green and climate-responsive skills Apprenticeship and dual training systems Skills certification aligned with AfCFTA 4. AI, Robotics, and Emerging Technologies Robotics labs in schools AI-powered skills forecasting tools Simulation-based technical training Youth innovation hubs AI ethics, governance, and data privacy solutions Eligibility Criteria Applicants must: Be citizens of an African Union Member State Lead a legally registered organization Be implementing an education innovation with verifiable results Be directly responsible for the innovation (not intermediaries) Demonstrate scalability and alignment with continental frameworks Note: Past IEA grant recipients are not eligible. Application Process Applications must be submitted online in English or French and include: Problem statement (100 words) Innovation description (500 words) Performance and impact report (500 words) Contribution to digital, green, AI, or skills ecosystems Proof of business registration Apply here Deadline: 30 April 2026, 23:00 EAT (GMT+3) Selection Process and Timeline The evaluation process includes: Eligibility Screening: 1–8 May 2026 Technical Review: 8–20 May 2026 Early Pitch (Virtual): May 2026 Final Pitch: June 2026 Final outcome: 50 shortlisted innovations 10 early pitch finalists 5 final pitch candidates 3 winners awarded grants Evaluation Criteria Submissions will be assessed based on: Innovation and uniqueness Scalability and sustainability Measurable impact Integration with public systems Industry relevance (for TVET) Gender and climate responsiveness Alignment with AU strategies (CESA, CTVET, STISA) Potential for commercialization and technology transfer Benefits for Selected Innovators Successful applicants will receive: Grants of up to USD 50,000 Recognition from the African Union Promotion across AU Member States and partners Publication in the Africa Education Skills Innovations Handbook 2026 Capacity-building support Participation in the IEA 2026 Expo and policy dialogues Opportunities to influence national education policies Inclusion in continental AI readiness initiatives Explore more details about the grant here The Innovating Education in Africa 2026 Call is more than a funding opportunity—it is a platform to shape the future of education across the continent . By supporting bold, scalable, and impactful solutions, the African Union aims to build inclusive, resilient, and future-ready education systems aligned with Africa’s long-term development goals. title: Apply Now: Join the US Refugee Advisory Board (USRAB) 2026 and Shape Global Refugee Policy
summary: Are you a leader with lived experience of forced displacement and a passion for influencing policy? The United States Refugee Advisory Board (USRAB) is now accepting applications for its third cohort of Advisors. This unique opportunity allows individuals to directly contribute to global and national policy discussions, ensuring that refugee voices are represented where decisions are made. Applications must be submitted by 31 March 2026 . About the Opportunity The United States Refugee Advisory Board (USRAB) is a self-governing initiative designed to ensure that forcibly displaced individuals have a meaningful and equitable role in policymaking processes . The board is coordinated by Refugee Congress , in partnership with Refugee Council USA (RCUSA) and the Institute for the Study of International Migration (ISIM) at Georgetown University . USRAB engages with a wide range of stakeholders, including the United States Government , the United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees (UNHCR) , civil society organizations, and refugee-led groups. Its mission is to ensure that the perspectives, knowledge, and lived experiences of displaced people are integrated into decision-making processes that affect their lives and communities. Selected Advisors will serve a three-year term , contributing to strategic discussions, policy engagement, and community consultations. The role is designed to create a transparent and accountable mechanism through which refugee voices can shape policies at both national and international levels. Why This Opportunity Matters Refugees and forcibly displaced individuals are often underrepresented in decision-making processes that directly impact their lives. The USRAB initiative addresses this gap by ensuring that those with lived experience are not only included but are central to shaping policies and programs. By creating a platform for meaningful participation, USRAB promotes equity, inclusion, and accountability in global migration and refugee governance. Advisors play a critical role in bridging the gap between policymakers and affected communities, ensuring that policies are informed by real experiences and practical insights. This opportunity is particularly impactful because it goes beyond consultation—it enables individuals to actively influence policy outcomes , contribute to strategic discussions, and engage in high-level dialogues with global stakeholders. What You’ll Gain Serving as a USRAB Advisor provides several unique benefits: Policy Influence: Directly contribute to shaping refugee and migration policies at national and global levels. Leadership Development: Strengthen your skills in advocacy, governance, and strategic engagement. Global Exposure: Engage with international organizations, policymakers, and civil society leaders. Networking Opportunities: Connect with a diverse community of advocates, experts, and stakeholders. Capacity Building: Participate in training and collaborative initiatives to enhance your impact. Although this is a volunteer position , all approved travel and participation costs related to USRAB activities are covered, ensuring accessibility for selected Advisors. Eligibility USRAB is seeking applicants who meet the following criteria: Individuals with lived experience of forced displacement are strongly encouraged to apply Demonstrated professional expertise or engagement in relevant fields such as advocacy, policy, or community work Ability to actively participate in meetings, consultations, and project-based work Willingness and ability to travel within the United States and internationally for meetings and events Availability to commit approximately 5–15 hours per month Applicants must also be able to participate in required training and engagements, as well as contribute meaningfully to the board’s work. Application Process and Deadline To apply for the USRAB Advisor cohort: Complete the Application Form: Fill out the official application form carefully. Prepare Required Documents: Submit your resume and two professional references . Submit Before Deadline: Ensure your application is complete and submitted on time. Incomplete applications will not be considered. Applications take approximately 30 minutes to complete. The original deadline has been extended to April 24th, 2026. To learn more, visit the official Refugee Congress website . You can find the form to apply here. title: Fully Funded Tufts One Health Fellowship Program 2026: A Global Health Leadership Opportunity in the United States
summary: Applications are now open for the Tufts One Health Fellowship Program 2026, a prestigious and fully funded global health leadership initiative hosted by Tufts University in collaboration with leading international partners. This highly competitive fellowship is designed to equip emerging professionals with the expertise, practical experience, and global networks required to tackle complex health challenges using a multidisciplinary approach. The program is built on the “One Health” concept, which recognizes the interconnectedness of human, animal, and environmental health. By integrating these sectors, fellows are trained to develop sustainable and innovative solutions to pressing global health issues. About the Tufts One Health Fellowship The Tufts One Health Fellowship is an intensive program that blends academic training, fieldwork, and professional development. It is specifically tailored for early- to mid-career professionals who are committed to improving public health systems in their home countries. Through a combination of coursework, mentorship, and real-world exposure, fellows gain the tools necessary to address health challenges that cross traditional disciplinary boundaries. Program Structure and Experience Participants in the fellowship benefit from a comprehensive learning journey that includes: Academic and Leadership Training Fellows receive advanced instruction in global health leadership, interdisciplinary collaboration, and systems thinking. The curriculum is designed to strengthen their ability to analyze and respond to complex health issues from multiple perspectives. Field Placement in the United States A key component of the program is a professional placement within a relevant organization in the United States. This hands-on experience allows fellows to work directly on real-world public health challenges, gaining practical skills and insights into international health systems. Mentorship and Networking Participants are paired with experienced global health professionals who provide guidance and mentorship throughout the program. Fellows also join a diverse international network of peers and alumni, fostering long-term collaboration and knowledge exchange. Benefits of the Fellowship The Tufts One Health Fellowship is fully funded, ensuring that selected participants can focus entirely on learning and professional growth. Benefits include: Full financial coverage of program participation costs Access to high-level global health training Placement opportunities in leading organizations in the United States Mentorship from experienced practitioners Networking opportunities with international experts and alumni Exposure to interdisciplinary approaches in global health This funding structure removes financial barriers and enables fellows to fully engage in the program experience. Eligibility Criteria Applicants must meet the following requirements: Be early- to mid-career professionals with a strong interest in public health Typically be between 25 and 40 years old (may vary slightly) Hold an academic or professional background in relevant fields such as: Medicine Veterinary science Environmental science Public health Social sciences Policy or related disciplines Have at least two years of relevant professional experience or active engagement in health-related work Demonstrate strong English communication skills Candidates should also show a clear commitment to improving health outcomes in their communities. Eligible Countries The Tufts One Health Fellowship is currently open to applicants from the following countries: Kenya Ethiopia Uganda Rwanda Applicants must be citizens or current residents of one of these countries. Eligibility may vary depending on the specific cohort, so candidates are encouraged to consult the official website for updates. Final Thoughts The Tufts One Health Fellowship Program 2026 represents a unique opportunity for emerging leaders in global health to gain world-class training, international exposure, and practical experience. By focusing on the intersection of human, animal, and environmental health, the program prepares fellows to become innovative problem-solvers capable of addressing some of the world’s most pressing health challenges. For professionals seeking to expand their impact and contribute to sustainable health solutions, this fellowship offers a powerful platform for growth and leadership. title: Internet Society Mid Career Fellowship 2026: Fully Funded Global Leadership Programme on Digital Transformation and Internet Governance

Scheduled for July 2026, this unique program brings together a diverse youth cast to explore one of the most pressing questions in modern sports: how big money is reshaping football fandom . This article provides a detailed overview of the program, including the debate theme, participation requirements, benefits, and how to apply. About the Doha Debates Town Hall Program The Doha Debates Town Hall is an interactive global platform that engages young people in meaningful discussions on complex global issues. Participants are selected to join a “youth cast” and take part in guided workshops, discussions, and a professionally produced town hall event. The Toronto edition builds on previous successful town halls, including one hosted in Buenos Aires, and aims to amplify youth voices on issues that intersect culture, economics, and identity. Debate Topic: Big Money and the Future of Football Fandom The 2026 town hall focuses on a timely and provocative topic: What does football fandom mean when big money is involved? Two decades ago, football clubs were often closely tied to local identity and community roots. Today, the landscape has shifted dramatically. Clubs like Manchester United are publicly traded and boast global fanbases numbering in the hundreds of millions, many of whom live far from the club’s home country. Despite this distance, emotional attachment remains strong. Fans celebrate victories, mourn defeats, and fiercely defend their teams online and offline. This raises important questions about identity and belonging in a globalized sports economy. Key Issues to Be Explored: The influence of private equity firms and sovereign wealth funds in football The rise of multi-club ownership models The globalization of fan communities The evolving meaning of loyalty and identity in sports Proponents argue that increased investment improves the quality and reach of the game. Critics, however, question how commercialization impacts authenticity and fan connection. The town hall will explore these perspectives through open dialogue and debate. Why You Should Apply Participating in the Doha Debates Town Hall offers: A platform to share your voice on a global stage Exposure to diverse perspectives on a major global issue Opportunities to build critical thinking and communication skills A chance to engage with a community of passionate, like-minded youth Participation Requirements To be considered for the program, applicants must meet the following requirements: Application Deadline Submit your application by April 16, 2026 Pre-Event Commitments Attend four virtual workshops (60–90 minutes each) between June 1 and June 26, 2026 Sessions will be scheduled after: 7:00 p.m. AST 4:00 p.m. EST Complete approximately 5 hours of required reading during the same period Availability for In-Person Program For participants based in Doha: Must be in Toronto from July 3 to July 10, 2026 Must have no conflicting commitments (including remote work or classes) For participants based in Toronto: Must be available from July 5 to July 8, 2026 Daily participation required between 9:00 a.m. and 9:00 p.m. Travel, Stipend, and Additional Considerations Travel and Accommodation Fully covered for participants traveling from Doha For participants already in Canada, support is provided on a case-by-case basis Stipend Selected participants will receive approximately: 375 CAD (or 1,000 QAR ) Media and Broadcasting The town hall will be professionally filmed Content will be published on Doha Debates platforms and may be broadcast more widely Important Notes for Applicants Doha Debates emphasizes curiosity, authenticity, and critical thinking. Applicants are encouraged to: Engage thoughtfully with the topic Demonstrate originality in their responses Use artificial intelligence tools responsibly and ethically when completing their application How to Apply Interested candidates should prepare their application materials and submit them before the deadline. Ensure you are able to meet all participation requirements before applying. Click here, to Register. This is more than just a discussion—it is an opportunity to examine how global forces are reshaping one of the world’s most beloved sports, and what that means for fans everywhere. If you are ready to contribute to this conversation, consider applying before the April 16 deadline. Discover more similar opportunities here. title: Apply for the Teaching with Primary Sources (TPS) Internship 2026 | Paid Library of Congress Internship
summary: The Teaching with Primary Sources (TPS) Internship Program offers a unique opportunity for students and recent graduates to gain hands-on experience in education, history, library science, and museum studies while working with one of the most prestigious knowledge institutions in the world. Hosted by the Office of the Librarian, this program provides both remote and onsite internship opportunities designed to support the educational use of primary sources. Program Overview The Teaching with Primary Sources (TPS) program is administered by the Professional Learning and Outreach Initiatives Office at the Library of Congress . It focuses on enhancing education through the use of digitized primary sources, offering teaching materials, professional development, and partnerships that support educators and learners. At the heart of the TPS initiative is the TPS Consortium—a nationwide network of partner organizations that develop educational programming and tools using the Library’s vast digital collections. This work aligns with the Library’s broader mission to engage, inspire, and inform the public through access to knowledge and creativity. The TPS Internship Program allows interns to actively contribute to this mission by supporting the development of educational materials and programming for K–12 audiences, educators, and families. Internship Structure and Opportunities Each semester, the program selects up to two interns: One remote intern One onsite intern based in Washington, DC Program Duration Short-term internships offered during Fall and Spring semesters Work Schedule Onsite Intern : Up to 35 hours per week (9:00 AM – 4:30 PM), five days a week, including Saturday Remote Intern : 20 hours per week (Monday–Friday), with flexible scheduling, but must overlap with 9:30 AM – 3:00 PM Eastern Time Compensation and Benefits The program offers competitive stipends depending on the internship format: Remote Internship $500 monthly stipend (20 hours/week) Onsite Internship $750 monthly stipend (35 hours/week) $255/month for local transportation Up to $1,250/month for housing support Travel allowance of up to $1,000 each way (for interns relocating to/from Washington, DC) Additional Benefits Access to Library of Congress internal discounts Academic Credit While the Library of Congress does not directly provide academic credit, interns may coordinate with their academic institutions in advance to receive credit for their participation. Why Apply? The TPS Internship Program is an excellent opportunity for aspiring educators, researchers, and cultural professionals to: Gain practical experience with primary source-based education Collaborate with experts and national education networks Contribute to meaningful educational content used across the United States Build a strong foundation for careers in education, libraries, museums, and public history Eligibility Criteria Applicants must meet the following requirements: Be a U.S. citizen or permanent resident Be at least 18 years old Be an undergraduate or graduate student in good standing , or a recent graduate (within six months) Demonstrate interest in: Education Library science History Museum studies Have experience (academic, volunteer, or professional) working with learners aged 9+, families, or K–12 educators Show strong collaboration and communication skills Key Responsibilities TPS interns play an active role in developing and supporting educational initiatives. Responsibilities include: Identifying primary source materials and effective learning strategies for K–12 audiences Learning about the Library’s collections, programs, and educational mission Assisting in planning and implementing educational programming Conducting research and consulting with Library experts Designing and executing a special project Contributing insights to the TPS Teachers Network Onsite Intern (Additional Responsibilities) Engage directly with young visitors (ages 9+) and families Develop and deliver in-person educational activities based on primary sources Application Process How to Apply Submit all required materials via email to: tpscolorado@msudenver.edu Subject line format: “TPS Internship – [Applicant’s Full Name]” Required Documents Resume Statement of purpose (250 words) outlining academic and career goals Project proposal (max 100 words) using digitized primary sources from the Library of Congress Transcript (unofficial accepted) Two academic or professional references (contact details only) Indication of availability (remote or onsite) Application Deadlines and Internship Dates Fall 2026 Application Period: March 2, 2026 – May 11, 2026 Internship Duration: Mid-September – Mid-December 2026 Important: All materials must be submitted by 11:59 PM (local time) on the deadline date. Late applications will not be considered. Note: The program does not provide feedback on individual applications, but general inquiries are welcome. The Teaching with Primary Sources Internship Program stands out as a highly enriching experience for individuals passionate about education and history. With both remote and onsite options, structured mentorship, and access to world-class resources, this internship offers a valuable pathway into impactful careers in education and cultural institutions. If you meet the eligibility criteria and are eager to work with primary sources to inspire learners, this program is well worth applying for. title: Chatham House Academy Fellowship 2026/2027 (Richard and Susan Hayden Fellowship): Fully Funded 10-Month Programme in London for Global Leaders in International Affairs
summary: The Chatham House Academy Fellowship 2026/2027, also known as the Richard and Susan Hayden Academy Fellowship, is a prestigious, fully funded international fellowship designed for early- to mid-career professionals from around the world. Hosted by Chatham House, one of the world’s leading policy institutes, this programme offers a unique opportunity to conduct independent research, develop leadership skills, and engage with global experts on pressing international issues. This fellowship is ideal for professionals working in academia, government, NGOs, business, civil society, or media who are committed to addressing global challenges and contributing to evidence-based policymaking. About Chatham House and the Academy Fellowship Chatham House, also known as The Royal Institute of International Affairs, is a globally recognized think tank dedicated to analyzing and addressing international challenges. Through its Academy, the institution develops future leaders by combining research, leadership training, and global networking. The Richard and Susan Hayden Academy Fellowship is a flagship programme that supports individuals in conducting independent research while actively contributing to Chatham House’s mission of building a secure, sustainable, and just world. Fellowship Overview The fellowship provides a structured and immersive experience combining research, leadership development, and institutional engagement. Key Details Programme Duration: 10 months Fellowship Period: October 2026 – July 2027 Location: Chatham House, London (with hybrid working options) Deadline: April 17, 2026 Eligibility: Open to applicants from all nationalities Fellows are expected to commit full-time to the programme and cannot undertake other work or studies simultaneously. Fellowship Structure The fellowship is divided into three main components, ensuring a balanced and impactful experience. 1. Independent Research Project (60%) Fellows design and conduct their own research project aligned with their expertise and interests. Key aspects include: Selection of a research topic relevant to international affairs Guidance from a Chatham House expert Development of policy-relevant outputs 2. Contribution to Chatham House Research (20%) Fellows actively participate in ongoing research initiatives within the institute. Activities include: Collaborating with research teams Supporting institutional research priorities Engaging in policy discussions and outputs 3. Leadership Masterclass Programme (20%) This core component focuses on building leadership capacity and professional development. Leadership Programme Components The leadership programme is designed to equip fellows with skills needed for global leadership. Key Elements Induction Week Five-day orientation programme Introduction to fellowship structure and expectations Thematic Sessions Discussions on global challenges Interaction with subject matter experts Leadership Skills Workshops Policy impact strategies Stakeholder engagement Crisis management Negotiation techniques Leadership Briefings Engagement with global leaders Insights into leadership strategies and experiences Project Presentations Fellows present research progress Peer review and feedback Personal Development Coaching One-on-one coaching sessions Goal setting and professional growth Media Training Interview skills for television and radio Mock interviews and feedback Career Mentoring (Optional) Mentorship opportunities Career guidance and development Financial Benefits and Support The fellowship provides comprehensive financial support to ensure participants can fully focus on their work. Financial Package Monthly stipend: £2,365 (to cover living expenses in London) Coverage of relocation costs: Flights and travel Temporary accommodation Visa-related expenses Payment of UK Immigration Healthcare Surcharge Funding for research activities: Fieldwork Conferences Publications Events Additional Benefits Access to Chatham House’s global network Participation in high-level policy discussions Opportunities to host research roundtables Five-year membership at Chatham House after completion Continued access to alumni and faculty networks Eligibility Criteria Who Can Apply Applicants from all countries (no nationality restrictions) Early- to mid-career professionals Required Qualifications Bachelor’s degree or equivalent Experience conducting research Professional background in: Academia NGOs Government Business Civil society Media Strong interest in international affairs Additional Requirements Demonstrated leadership potential Commitment to Chatham House’s mission Ability to communicate effectively in English Why You Should Apply This fellowship offers a rare opportunity to: Conduct independent research at a leading global think tank Develop leadership and policy skills Engage with international experts and policymakers Expand your professional network Contribute to solving global challenges Application Requirements Applicants must submit: CV or résumé Names of two referees Research proposal aligned with fellowship themes Selection Criteria Candidates are evaluated based on: Research experience and academic background Leadership potential Relevance of proposed research Motivation and commitment Ability to create positive impact Selection Process Stage 1 Application review and shortlisting Stage 2 Interview via Zoom with senior management Successful candidates will be notified via email. Learn more about the The Chatham House Academy Fellowship HERE. Application Deadline The deadline to apply for the Chatham House Academy Fellowship 2026/2027 is: April 17, 2026 Applicants are encouraged to submit early to ensure a complete and competitive application. See our Careers page for full details. Discover more global Fellowship opportunities HERE. title: UNFPA Remote Internships 2026 (People Strategy Roles): Complete Guide to People Analytics, HR Policy & Leadership Development Opportunities
summary: The United Nations Population Fund (UNFPA) is offering highly competitive remote internship opportunities for 2026 within its Division for Human Resources (DHR). These internships are part of UNFPA’s evolving People Strategy and align with its 2026–2029 Strategic Plan, focusing on digital transformation, workforce planning, and organizational development. This comprehensive internship programme includes three specialized tracks: People Analytics AI, Human Resources Policy, and Learning Leadership Development. In addition to gaining practical experience, applicants must complete a structured application process through the official UNFPA People Strategy Internship Application Form. This article provides a complete, detailed breakdown of the internship roles, eligibility, responsibilities, application process, and additional requirements. About UNFPA and Its Global Mission UNFPA is the United Nations agency dedicated to ensuring reproductive health, gender equality, and population data systems worldwide. Its mission centers on three transformative goals: Ending preventable maternal deaths Ending unmet need for family planning Ending gender-based violence and harmful practices Through its strategic framework, UNFPA supports countries in achieving sustainable development goals and adapting to demographic changes using evidence-based policies. Overview of the UNFPA 2026 People Strategy Internships UNFPA’s Division for Human Resources is recruiting interns across three strategic functional areas. Available Internship Tracks People Analytics AI Intern Human Resources Policy Intern Learning Leadership Development Intern General Internship Details Location: Remote (with optional in-person placement in New York or Nairobi if locally recruited) Duration: 6 months Schedule: Full-time Timeline: Expected between April and December 2026 Organizational Unit: Division for Human Resources (DHR) Interns will work under structured supervision, with defined work plans, continuous feedback, and performance evaluation. Internship Track 1: People Analytics AI Intern This role is focused on transforming HR through data, automation, and artificial intelligence. Key Responsibilities Collect, clean, and analyze workforce data across recruitment, performance, and mobility Maintain HR dashboards using Power BI Translate complex datasets into actionable insights for leadership Automate processes using AI tools such as Gemini Develop AI prompts for policy analysis and sentiment evaluation Monitor HR KPIs and troubleshoot data systems Prepare reports and presentations for diverse audiences Required Skills Experience with spreadsheets (Excel, Google Sheets) Familiarity with data visualization tools (Power BI, Tableau, Looker) Basic knowledge of SQL or Python (preferred) Experience with generative AI tools (ChatGPT, Gemini) Strong analytical and storytelling skills Learn more here Internship Track 2: Human Resources Policy Intern This role focuses on HR governance, policy design, and organizational systems. Key Responsibilities Support development and revision of HR policies and procedures Conduct research on HR trends and best practices across the UN system Analyze and recommend policy improvements Assist in policy implementation and communication Draft briefing notes, reports, and position papers Manage knowledge systems and internal documentation Collaborate with stakeholders across UN agencies Required Skills Strong analytical and writing skills Interest in HR policy and governance Research and documentation capabilities Ability to work collaboratively in multicultural teams Learn more here Internship Track 3: Learning Leadership Development Intern This role focuses on training, onboarding, and leadership capacity development. Key Responsibilities Support onboarding and leadership programmes Coordinate learning activities and track participation Develop training materials and toolkits Conduct research on talent development trends Support analytics and dashboard creation Use AI tools to enhance learning systems Prepare communication and reporting materials Required Skills Interest in learning and development Experience with digital tools and AI technologies Strong communication and analytical abilities Experience working in global or remote teams Learn more here Eligibility Criteria Applicants must meet at least one of the following: Be enrolled in a postgraduate programme (Master’s or higher) Be in the final year of a bachelor’s degree Have graduated within the last 12 months Be enrolled in a postgraduate traineeship programme Additional Requirements Fluency in English Strong communication and teamwork skills Ability to work in multicultural environments Application Process: UNFPA People Strategy Internship Form Applicants must complete the official UNFPA application form and provide detailed information across multiple sections. Section 1: Personal Information Applicants must provide: Full name Email address Nationality Country of residence Work location (city/country) Availability (April–December 2026) Time zone Age Section 2: Eligibility Screening Applicants must confirm: Student status or recent graduation (within 12 months) Highest level of education (Bachelor, Master, PhD) Current or ongoing education English proficiency Experience in HR, learning, or talent management Experience with UN or international institutions (if any) Field of study Experience working with global or remote teams Required Application Materials Applicants must submit: CV Cover letter Additional supporting documents (optional) LinkedIn profile (optional) Video Requirement Applicants must upload a short video (maximum 2 minutes) answering: Introduction Motivation for applying to UNFPA HR Division Relevant experience and contributions Motivation Statement Applicants must provide a written statement explaining: Why they are applying What value they will bring to the team Application Deadline The deadline to apply for the UNFPA People Strategy Internships is: April 19, 2026 Applicants are strongly encouraged to apply early and ensure all documents are accessible. title: Fully Funded EU4Ocean Summer School 2026 at the Mediterranean Sea and Black Sea
summary: If you are a student or early-career professional looking for a fully funded international opportunity in 2026, the EU4Ocean Summer School on Ocean Literacy Collaboration is one of the most prestigious global programs you should not miss. This opportunity offers participants the chance to travel, learn, network, and contribute to global ocean sustainability efforts—all in an immersive academic and professional environment. What is the EU4Ocean Summer School? The EU4Ocean Summer School is an international training program focused on ocean literacy, sustainability, and collaboration. It brings together a small, highly competitive group of participants from across the world to engage in interdisciplinary learning and problem-solving. title: HCoC Youth Group Fellowship 2026–2027: Fully Funded Global Programme on Missile Non-Proliferation and Security Studies
summary: The HCoC Youth Group Fellowship 2026–2027 is a prestigious international programme designed for young professionals, graduate students, and early-career researchers interested in global security, arms control, and non-proliferation. Organized by the Fondation pour la Recherche Stratégique (FRS) with support from the European Union, this fellowship provides a unique platform to engage with leading experts, participate in high-level discussions, and contribute to policy-relevant research on ballistic missile proliferation. This second edition builds on the success of its inaugural cohort and responds to an increasingly complex global security environment characterized by rapid technological advancements, shifting geopolitical dynamics, and the growing spread of missile technologies. About the HCoC Youth Group Programme The HCoC Youth Group is part of a broader initiative supporting the Hague Code of Conduct against Ballistic Missile Proliferation (HCoC), an international effort aimed at promoting transparency, confidence-building, and restraint in missile development. The programme is structured as an 18-month fellowship that combines: Virtual learning and discussions In-person engagement with experts Independent research and publication Participants will join a global cohort of 15 fellows, creating a diverse and interdisciplinary learning environment. Programme Structure and Activities The HCoC Youth Group Fellowship offers a comprehensive and interactive learning experience. 1. Expert Webinars Fellows will participate in four thematic webinars scheduled across the programme timeline: November 2026 February 2027 April 2027 June 2027 Each session includes: Expert presentations by researchers, diplomats, and practitioners Moderated discussions Pre-submitted questions from fellows These sessions provide deep insights into missile non-proliferation and related global security issues. 2. Virtual Cafés Optional informal sessions will be held throughout the programme to: Foster peer interaction and collaboration Discuss current global developments Share research progress and ideas These sessions promote a collaborative and supportive learning environment. 3. In-Person Workshop (Lithuania) A key highlight of the programme is a three-day residential workshop scheduled for September 2027 in Plokštinė, Lithuania (subject to confirmation). Key features include: Expert briefings and discussions Research exchange sessions Simulation exercises (e.g., negotiation scenarios or war games) Guided visit to a former Cold War missile base All travel and accommodation costs for this workshop are fully covered by the programme. 4. Individual Research and Publication Each fellow is required to produce an individual research paper as part of the programme. Key aspects include: Selection of a research topic aligned with programme themes Development of an analytical paper Contribution to a collective publication edited by FRS This publication serves as a key output of the programme and enhances participants’ academic and professional profiles. Thematic Pillars The programme is structured around two core thematic pillars: Pillar 1: Technical Dimension Focus areas include: Emerging trends in missile proliferation Dual-use technologies and space systems Intersections between civilian and military technologies Implications for arms control frameworks Pillar 2: Diplomatic Dimension Focus areas include: Evaluation of the Hague Code of Conduct International cooperation and policy responses Challenges in a multipolar geopolitical landscape Recommendations for strengthening global non-proliferation efforts Applicants are required to indicate their preferred thematic focus during the application process. Eligibility Criteria The programme is open to a diverse group of candidates with relevant academic or professional backgrounds. Who Should Apply Young professionals Graduate students (Master’s level or above) Early-career researchers Required Qualifications Demonstrated interest in one or more of the following areas: Ballistic missile technology Arms control and strategic studies WMD non-proliferation Space policy and governance International law and diplomacy Regional security studies Full working proficiency in English Availability to participate in all programme activities Age requirement: Applicants must be under 40 years old Selection Criteria The selection committee will evaluate candidates based on: Quality and clarity of application Relevance of academic or professional background Geographic diversity Gender balance Interdisciplinary representation Time Commitment The programme is designed to be flexible while maintaining high engagement. Estimated commitment: 4–6 hours per month Additional time required for research and workshop preparation Why You Should Apply This fellowship provides a rare opportunity to engage with global security issues at a high level while developing research, analytical, and policy skills. It is particularly valuable for individuals aiming to build careers in international relations, defense policy, diplomacy, or strategic studies. Application Process Applicants must submit: Required Materials Completed online application form Curriculum Vitae (maximum 2 pages) Application Questions Include Motivation for joining the programme Key challenges in missile proliferation Contribution to the group Thematic interest A key question for experts All submissions must be in English. Application Deadline The deadline to apply for the HCoC Youth Group Fellowship 2026–2027 is: July 31, 2026 Applicants will be notified of the outcome within four weeks after the deadline. Find the Call for Applications document here . Applications must be submitted through the online form available here. Programme Benefits Participation in the programme offers numerous benefits, including: Free participation (no programme fees) Fully funded travel and accommodation for the in-person workshop Access to leading experts and policymakers Opportunity to publish research work Global networking and collaboration Practical experience in international security and diplomacy Terms and Conditions Fellows participate in a personal capacity All research outputs will be published under participants’ names Active participation throughout the programme is mandatory Non-participation may lead to removal from the programme Contact Information For inquiries, applicants can contact: m.garcia-carrasco@frstrategie.org Learn more about the HCoC Youth Group Fellowship HERE. Discover more global Fellowship and Research opportunities on OFY. title: BIGS-DR Scholarships 2026 at ZEF, University of Bonn: Fully Funded PhD Opportunity for Global South Researchers in Sustainable Development
summary: The Center for Development Research (ZEF) at the University of Bonn is inviting applications for the prestigious BIGS-DR Scholarships 2026. title: GAIN Mentor Program 2026/2027: Volunteer to Guide African Students into Graduate School (Global Opportunity)
summary: The Graduate Application International Network (GAIN) Mentor Program 2026/2027 is a unique global volunteer opportunity designed for experienced academics and professionals who are passionate about increasing African representation in graduate education. This impactful mentorship initiative connects mentors with aspiring African students, guiding them through the complex graduate application process in fields such as economics, public policy, political science, and public health. By becoming a GAIN mentor, you will play a direct role in shaping the academic and professional futures of talented African students while contributing to a broader mission of equity and inclusion in global academia. About the GAIN Program The GAIN program is a structured, multi-phase initiative that supports African students throughout their graduate application journey. It combines educational webinars, personalized mentorship, and financial support to reduce barriers to higher education. The program is divided into three main components: Webinar Program Mentoring Program Alumni Support Network Each component is designed to progressively equip participants with the knowledge, resources, and confidence needed to successfully apply to graduate schools worldwide. The Role of a GAIN Mentor GAIN mentors are at the heart of the program. They provide personalized guidance and support to mentees navigating the graduate application process. Core Responsibilities Mentors typically: Guide one mentee through the entire application cycle Conduct regular virtual meetings (usually bi-weekly) Provide feedback on application materials such as CVs and statements of purpose Offer advice on selecting universities and programs Support preparation for standardized tests such as GRE and TOEFL Share personal experiences and insights from their academic journeys In addition to one-on-one mentoring, some mentors may choose to: Support group sessions or workshops Offer expertise in specific areas such as scholarship applications or essay editing Participate in collaborative mentor networks or peer-support groups Time Commitment and Duration Mentors are expected to commit a moderate but consistent amount of time throughout the program. Key Details Commitment: Approximately 4–8 hours per month Duration: May/June 2026 – April 2027 Format: Fully virtual engagement This flexible structure allows mentors to balance their professional responsibilities while making a meaningful impact. Who Typically Becomes a GAIN Mentor GAIN mentors are usually individuals who have successfully navigated graduate education themselves and are willing to support others on the same path. Typical Mentor Profiles PhD students Academic researchers University professors Policy professionals and advisors Most mentors have firsthand experience with graduate applications and understand the challenges involved. Eligibility Requirements for Mentors To register as a GAIN mentor, applicants must meet the following criteria: Required Criteria Ability to commit 4–8 hours per month throughout the mentoring cycle Experience completing a graduate application process in: Economics Public policy Political science Public health Or related disciplines Strong interest in increasing African representation in academia Willingness to guide and support mentees consistently Preferred Criteria Previous mentoring experience Strong communication and interpersonal skills Commitment to diversity, inclusion, and academic equity Overview of the GAIN Program Structure Understanding the full GAIN program helps mentors better support their mentees. 1. Webinar Program (January – June) This foundational phase provides participants with essential knowledge about the graduate application process. Topics covered include: Scholarships and funding opportunities GRE, TOEFL, and standardized test strategies Writing academic CVs and statements of purpose Securing letters of recommendation Choosing universities and career paths Balancing academic pursuits with personal responsibilities Webinars are held every two weeks and feature leading African scholars and experts. 2. Mentoring Program This is the core phase where mentors actively engage with mentees. Key features include: One-on-one mentor-mentee matching based on: Field of study Professional background Country of origin Bi-weekly meetings and continuous support Guidance on application preparation and submission The mentoring phase begins with a GRE summer bootcamp, providing participants with access to preparation materials and structured support. 3. Financial Support for Mentees GAIN provides financial assistance to selected participants to reduce barriers to application. Support includes: Application fee coverage Standardized test fees (GRE, TOEFL) Score submission costs High-performing mentees may qualify for Mentoring+, which offers: Increased financial support Enhanced mentorship engagement 4. GAIN Alumni Network After successful admission into graduate programs, mentees transition into the GAIN Alumni Network. Benefits include: Monthly check-in sessions Continued mentorship and guidance Targeted webinars and academic support Community building and networking opportunities This ensures long-term success beyond admission. Why You Should Become a GAIN Mentor Becoming a GAIN mentor is not only an opportunity to give back but also a chance to grow professionally and personally. Key Benefits Contribute to increasing African representation in global academia Support talented students in achieving their academic goals Build meaningful professional relationships Gain mentorship and leadership experience Join a global network of academics and professionals Application Process To become a GAIN mentor: Complete the Online Mentor Registration Form Provide details about your academic and professional background Indicate your availability and areas of expertise Application Deadline The deadline to register as a GAIN Mentor for the 2026/2027 cycle is: April 30, 2026 Applicants are encouraged to apply early to secure placement in the program. Click HERE to learn more about the GAIN Mentor Program 2026/2027. Discover more global Mentorship and African opportunities on OFY. title: Researcher/Writer – Climate Adaptation & Resilience : Global Remote Opportunity with the Institute for Human Rights and Business (IHRB)
summary: The Institute for Human Rights and Business is currently seeking 1–2 experienced Researchers/Writers specializing in climate adaptation and resilience to lead a new phase of its flagship JUST Stories project. This is a unique global opportunity for professionals who combine rigorous research expertise with exceptional storytelling skills and a deep understanding of climate justice. This role sits at the intersection of climate action, human rights, and global development, offering the chance to contribute to a transformative initiative aimed at reshaping how climate adaptation is understood, financed, and implemented worldwide. Application Deadline and Timeline Applications must be submitted by Wednesday, April 15, 2026 at 11:59 PM (UK time) . Shortlisted candidates will be invited to: First-round interviews: April 23–24, 2026 Second-round interviews: Scheduled the following week The expected start date is April or May 2026 , with the contract running through September 2027 . About the JUST Stories Project The JUST Stories initiative, launched in 2024, is a global effort to identify and document real-world examples of effective, people-centered climate action. While the first phase focused on decarbonization, this second phase shifts attention toward climate adaptation and resilience. The project is grounded in a critical insight: climate solutions fail when they exclude the communities most affected. By contrast, successful adaptation emerges from inclusive, locally grounded, and co-designed approaches. Over the next 18 months, the project will: Identify and document community-led adaptation solutions worldwide Highlight best practices and enabling conditions Translate these stories into actionable insights for policymakers, investors, and practitioners These outputs are not merely narratives but serve as evidence-based tools to inform decision-making across finance, policy, and corporate sectors. Role Overview The selected Researchers/Writers will play a central role in shaping this next phase by producing immersive, field-based stories that bridge the gap between local realities and global decision-making systems. The role requires the ability to operate across two critical dimensions: Ground-level engagement with communities and frontline actors System-level understanding of finance, governance, and climate policy Key Responsibilities Research and Analysis Conduct in-depth literature reviews on climate adaptation, resilience, and locally-led approaches Identify high-impact case studies across diverse global contexts Develop frameworks to assess effective and equitable adaptation Fieldwork and Primary Research Design and conduct interviews with community leaders, institutions, and workers Undertake international field visits to gather firsthand data and narratives Ensure ethical research practices, including consent and safeguarding Narrative Development Produce high-quality, publication-ready stories integrating human experience and analytical insight Translate complex findings into clear, actionable recommendations Collaborate with communications teams to create multi-format outputs (articles, briefings, audio, visual content) Strategic Contribution Support cross-sector engagement involving policymakers, investors, and civil society Contribute to synthesizing lessons across case studies Participate in global convenings such as Climate Weeks and COP events Required Expertise and Skills Applicants must demonstrate: At least 7 years of experience in research and writing Proven expertise in climate adaptation and resilience Strong background in qualitative research and fieldwork Exceptional storytelling and analytical writing abilities Understanding of global frameworks such as UN climate and human rights standards Experience in working with Indigenous communities, NGOs, or climate finance actors is highly valued. Contract Details and Compensation Type: Consultancy contract under UK law Duration: April/May 2026 – September 2027 Compensation: Approximately £70,000 total budget Structure Options: One full-time lead researcher/writer Two part-time contributors Candidates must: Have the legal right to work in their country Provide their own equipment and internet access Be available for international travel (up to three times per year) Work Environment This is a fully remote role open globally. However, candidates must be located in time zones compatible with Central European Time (CET) or westward to ensure collaboration with teams operating on UK and West Africa time. Why This Opportunity Matters This role represents more than a writing or research position. It is an opportunity to directly influence how climate adaptation is conceptualized and implemented globally. By documenting and amplifying community-led solutions, the selected candidates will help shift dominant narratives away from top-down approaches toward inclusive, effective, and sustainable climate action. Professionals passionate about climate justice, human rights, and impactful storytelling are strongly encouraged to apply. title: NBCUniversal Local Impact Grants 2026: $2.5 Million Funding Opportunity for Nonprofits Supporting Youth, Storytelling, and Community Engagement
summary: The NBCUniversal Local Impact Grants 2026 program is now officially open, offering a significant funding opportunity for nonprofit organizations working to strengthen local communities. With a total of $2.5 million in grants available, this initiative reflects NBCUniversal’s commitment to empowering grassroots organizations and fostering sustainable community development. This annual grant program is designed to support nonprofit organizations operating in select markets where NBC and Telemundo-owned television stations are present. By providing unrestricted funding, NBCUniversal ensures that recipient organizations have the flexibility to allocate resources where they are most needed, enabling them to maximize their impact. Application Timeline and Deadline Applications for the 2026 NBCUniversal Local Impact Grants will be accepted from March 24, 2026, through April 24, 2026 . All applications must be submitted before 7:59:59 PM Eastern Time on April 24, 2026 . Late submissions will not be considered under any circumstances, making it essential for applicants to plan and submit their proposals well in advance of the deadline. Funding Overview NBCUniversal will award grants to eligible nonprofit organizations with annual operating expenses ranging between $100,000 and $1,000,000 . The grants are categorized as general operating funds , meaning recipients are not restricted to specific project expenditures. Instead, funding can support broader organizational needs, including staffing, program expansion, infrastructure, and operational sustainability. Key Focus Areas The NBCUniversal Local Impact Grants program prioritizes organizations that are actively working within three major thematic areas: 1. Youth Education and Empowerment This category supports programs that equip young people with essential tools for success. Initiatives may include: STEM and STEAM education programs Youth entrepreneurship development In-school and community-based educational support Skill-building and leadership development initiatives Organizations that focus on preparing youth for future academic and professional success are strongly encouraged to apply. 2. Next Generation Storytellers This category is dedicated to nurturing emerging voices and creative talent. Eligible programs include: Media and communications training Arts, journalism, and storytelling initiatives Opportunities in sports and entertainment industries Platforms that amplify youth voices and perspectives This focus area aims to diversify and strengthen the pipeline of talent in storytelling and media industries. 3. Community Engagement Programs under this category aim to strengthen social cohesion and civic participation. Examples include: Volunteerism initiatives Community-building events and programs Projects that encourage local engagement and collaboration Efforts to foster unity and inclusivity within communities Organizations working to create meaningful connections and engagement at the community level will find strong alignment with this category. Eligibility Criteria To qualify for the NBCUniversal Local Impact Grants, applicants must: Be a registered nonprofit organization Operate within one of the eligible NBC or Telemundo station markets Have total annual expenses between $100,000 and $1,000,000 Demonstrate measurable impact within their local community Align their work with at least one of the three focus areas Application Process Interested organizations must complete their applications through the official NBCUniversal Local Impact Grants portal. Additionally, applicants are encouraged to register for informational webinars hosted by NBCUniversal to gain insights into the application process, evaluation criteria, and best practices for submission. Applicants should ensure that their proposals clearly articulate their mission, impact, and alignment with NBCUniversal’s priority areas. Strong applications typically highlight measurable outcomes, community relevance, and sustainability. Why This Grant Matters The NBCUniversal Local Impact Grants stand out due to their flexibility and emphasis on local impact. By offering unrestricted funding, the program recognizes the importance of empowering nonprofits to address community needs in dynamic and adaptive ways. Moreover, the focus on youth, storytelling, and community engagement reflects a forward-looking approach to social development—one that values innovation, inclusivity, and grassroots leadership. Organizations seeking to expand their reach, strengthen their operations, or deepen their community impact should strongly consider applying for this opportunity.
